title: The State Secret
summary: A scientist leaked classified research, claiming the public had a right to know about hidden risks.

preview.summary: A scientist leaked classified research, claiming the public had a right to know about hidden risks.
preview.charge: A scientist is accused of illegal disclosure of classified national-security research.
preview.evidence: The prosecution relies on the scientist’s admissions, document tracing, and downstream exposure of classified material.
preview.complication: The defense argues public-interest necessity and disputes whether downstream foreign access should be attributed to the scientist.

policeReport: Federal agents traced a classified research packet from a secure materials lab to an encrypted archive later accessed by a journalist and, weeks afterward, by foreign analysts. The defendant admitted disclosing the material to expose concealed safety risks, but denied any intent to aid a foreign power or broaden the leak beyond the initial recipient.

witnesses.name: Dr. Iris Vale
witnesses.statement: I released the report because the public was being kept in the dark about a serious hazard. I did not intend for any foreign government to receive it, and I only shared it with a domestic journalist.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Critical first-hand witness, but her justification is self-protective and contested.

witnesses.name: Owen Grant
witnesses.statement: The classified packet that appeared in foreign monitoring channels matched the research version removed from the lab. Once the defendant broke the security chain, exposure became foreseeable.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Strong on chain-of-custody analysis, though he cannot prove who redistributed the file after the first leak.

witnesses.name: Nadia Cho
witnesses.statement: The defendant said the report showed risks that officials were burying. I received the material through a secure channel and redacted portions before publication.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Direct evidence of the initial disclosure, but not of later foreign access.

evidence.description: An internal access log showing the defendant exported the classified report shortly before contacting the journalist.
evidence.relevance: Directly ties the defendant to the initial removal of the document.

evidence.description: Messages discussing hidden safety risks, source protection, and the urgency of disclosure.
evidence.relevance: Supports motive and intent to leak, but also supports a whistleblowing narrative.

evidence.description: Foreign-accessed files contained matching section markers and formatting signatures from the exported lab report.
evidence.relevance: Supports the claim that the same classified material eventually reached foreign analysts.

priorRulings.title: United States v. Halden
priorRulings.relevance: A public-interest motive does not automatically negate liability for deliberate disclosure of classified national-security material.

prosecutionObjective: Prove the scientist knowingly broke classification controls and set in motion a disclosure chain that exposed sensitive research beyond any claimed whistleblowing purpose.
defenseObjective: Show the leak was a constrained public-interest disclosure, that later foreign access was an independent downstream act, and that the prosecution overstates intent and causation.
